docs.title: AI Safe Charity Index
docs.text:
Practical, evidence-led guidance to help UK charities use AI safely, confidently and responsibly.

The aim is simple: help charities save time with AI without risking people's data, trust or safety.

docs.title: Start With the Core Check
docs.text:
Use the Before You Paste framework before putting information into an AI tool.

It helps you decide whether a task is:

  • Green: go ahead with normal checks
  • Amber: pause and check
  • Red: stop

docs.title: What This Site Will Cover
docs.text:
• safer use of AI in everyday charity work
  • GDPR and data-protection basics
  • safeguarding considerations
  • trustee and governance guidance
  • common AI tool guidance
  • templates, checklists and review processes
  • updates, evidence and release notes

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text: This site is in its foundation build. Pages marked as draft are being converted from the project specification corpus and should be reviewed before being treated as final guidance.

docs.title: Templates
docs.text: This section will include reusable AI policy, staff rules, trustee briefing, approved-tools register, AI-use register, tool-assessment template, output-review checklist, incident form and DPIA triage resources.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index is an open, living resource for charities that want to use artificial intelligence responsibly.

It translates complex guidance into practical steps that charity staff, volunteers, managers and trustees can understand and use.

The project is guided by evidence, plain English, charity relevance, accessibility and human accountability.

docs.title: Creator
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew James Nel.

Connect with Matthew on LinkedIn

docs.title: Open Collaboration
docs.text:
The project invites constructive edits from collaborators who can improve the guidance, sources, accessibility, plain-English content, templates or charity relevance.

Researchers, practitioners, trustees, charity staff, data-protection professionals and safeguarding specialists are also invited to contribute evidence and research on AI policy in charities.

See the Contact page for collaboration and research topics.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew James Nel.

The project is being developed as an open, evidence-led resource for UK charities, and constructive edits, corrections and research contributions are welcome.

Connect with Matthew on LinkedIn

docs.title: Collaborate on the Knowledge Base
docs.text:
You can help improve the project by:

  • suggesting edits to public guidance
  • flagging unclear or outdated content
  • sharing reliable sources
  • reviewing pages for charity relevance
  • reviewing pages for accessibility and plain English
  • suggesting practical examples for small and medium charities
  • improving templates, checklists and governance tools

Please avoid sharing personal data, safeguarding information, confidential charity material, passwords, API keys or private case details.

docs.title: Research Collaboration
docs.text:
Matthew welcomes conversations with people interested in research on:

  • AI policy in charities
  • responsible AI governance for small and medium charities
  • trustee oversight of AI
  • charity staff confidence and training needs
  • AI, safeguarding and service delivery
  • AI and data protection in practical charity workflows
  • tool adoption, risk assessment and approved-tools processes

The project is especially interested in research that can help charities make safer, clearer and more proportionate decisions about AI.

docs.title: Corrections and Feedback
docs.text:
If you spot an error or want to suggest a source, please include:

  • the page title or URL
  • what should change
  • the source or evidence supporting the change
  • whether the issue is urgent or high risk

Do not use GitHub issues or public project spaces to share personal data, safeguarding information, confidential charity material, API keys or passwords.

docs.title: Can I paste personal data into an approved business AI account?
docs.text: Not automatically. Approval of the tool is only one condition. The specific use must also be necessary, lawful, minimised, transparent, secure and within the charity's policy and contract.

docs.title: Is a paid personal account safe for charity work?
docs.text: Payment alone does not create business terms, a Data Processing Agreement or admin controls. Use the charity's approved organisational account.

docs.title: Does removing a name make information anonymous?
docs.text: Not necessarily. A unique story or combination of details may still identify someone, and pseudonymised data can remain personal data.

docs.title: Can AI take meeting notes?
docs.text: Sometimes, but recording and transcription change the data-processing risk. Give appropriate notice, determine the lawful and fair basis, restrict access and retention, and avoid sensitive meetings unless specifically approved.

docs.title: Can trustees use AI to make decisions?
docs.text: AI can assist, but trustees remain responsible and should not rely on AI output alone for material decisions.

docs.title: Are AI citations reliable?
docs.text: No citation should be trusted without opening the source and confirming that it supports the exact claim.

docs.title: Agentic AI
docs.text: AI that can plan steps and act through tools or connected systems, not only produce an answer.

docs.title: Automated Decision
docs.text: A decision produced by automated processing. Legal rules become especially important where there is no meaningful human involvement and the effect is significant.

docs.title: Connector
docs.text: A link that lets an AI retrieve information from or act in another service, such as email, file storage, CRM or project-management software.

docs.title: Controller
docs.text: The organisation that decides why and how personal data is processed.

docs.title: Data Minimisation
docs.text: Using only the personal data genuinely needed for the purpose.

docs.title: DPIA
docs.text: A Data Protection Impact Assessment. A structured assessment required before processing likely to create high risk to people.

docs.title: Human Oversight
docs.text: Competent human involvement that can identify problems and change the result.

docs.title: Prompt Injection
docs.text: Instructions hidden in content that try to make an AI system reveal information or act unexpectedly.

docs.title: Pseudonymisation
docs.text: Replacing identifiers while keeping separate information that could reconnect the data to a person. Pseudonymised data can remain personal data.

docs.title: Synthetic Media
docs.text: AI-generated or altered image, audio or video.

docs.title: Accessibility
docs.text:
The project aims to meet WCAG 2.2 AA wherever practical.

Accessibility is part of editorial quality, technical quality and public trust. Guidance should use clear headings, descriptive links, plain English, accessible tables, meaningful alt text and text labels that do not rely on colour alone.

Green, Amber and Red guidance must always include text labels and explanations, not colour alone.

docs.title: AI and Data Protection
docs.text:
This section explains the data-protection concepts charities need when using AI tools.

UK data-protection law is technology-neutral. If an AI use processes personal data, the charity still needs to meet the usual principles: lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimisation, accuracy, storage limitation, security and accountability.

docs.title: July 2026 Position
docs.text:
The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 amended the UK GDPR, Data Protection Act 2018 and PECR. The ICO says all data-protection provisions in the DUAA are now in force.

The DUAA does not make AI automatically safe or compliant. It changes some rules and creates some opportunities, but charities still need a lawful basis, fairness, transparency, security, minimisation and safeguards.

docs.title: What Charities Should Check
docs.text:
Before using AI with personal data, check:

  • what personal data is involved
  • whether special category or criminal-offence data is involved
  • whether children or adults at risk could be affected
  • whether the use is necessary and proportionate
  • whether the tool and account are approved
  • whether a Data Processing Agreement or other contract is needed
  • whether the use is likely to need a DPIA
  • whether people need to be told about the use
  • whether the output could affect someone significantly

docs.title: Personal Data and Pseudonymisation
docs.text:
Removing a name does not automatically make information anonymous. A distinctive story, rare role, location, condition, incident or combination of details can still identify someone.

Pseudonymised information can remain personal data where it can be reconnected to a person.

docs.title: Pages in This Section
docs.text:
• DPIA triage for AI
  • Automated decisions and profiling

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Guidance on AI and data protection
  • ICO: The Data Use and Access Act 2025: what does it mean for organisations?
  • Data (Use and Access) Act 2025

docs.title: Automated Decisions and Profiling
docs.text:
Interim guidance

The ICO says updated automated decision-making and profiling guidance is in drafting, with final guidance due in winter 2026. Treat this page as interim and seek data-protection or legal review before using AI for significant decisions about people.

AI can support decisions, but it must not quietly become the decision-maker for important outcomes.

docs.title: Assistive Use
docs.text: AI helps a person draft, summarise or organise information. A competent person makes the final judgement.

docs.title: Recommendation or Scoring
docs.text: AI ranks, scores, predicts or recommends something about a person. This is higher risk and may need a DPIA.

docs.title: Significant Automated Decision
docs.text: AI or automated processing makes a decision with legal or similarly significant effects without meaningful human involvement. This is Red until reviewed.

docs.title: Examples That Need Care
docs.text:
• recruitment scoring or filtering
  • service eligibility decisions
  • grant or support prioritisation
  • disciplinary or grievance decisions
  • risk scoring for beneficiaries
  • donor profiling that affects treatment or contact
  • decisions involving children or adults at risk

docs.title: Meaningful Human Involvement
docs.text: Human involvement should be real. The person reviewing the output should have enough information, skill and authority to challenge or change the result.

docs.title: Safeguards to Consider
docs.text:
• DPIA before deployment
  • clear explanation to affected people
  • human intervention route
  • challenge or appeal route
  • bias and accuracy testing
  • monitoring after launch
  • clear record of responsibility
  • special review where special category data is involved

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: The Data Use and Access Act 2025: what does it mean for organisations?
  • ICO: Technology guidance plan
  • Data (Use and Access) Act 2025

docs.title: DPIA Triage for AI
docs.text:
A Data Protection Impact Assessment is required before processing that is likely to result in a high risk to people's rights and freedoms.

This page is a triage aid, not a completed DPIA.

docs.title: Start With These Questions
docs.text:
Ask whether the AI use involves:

  • personal data
  • special category data
  • criminal-offence data
  • children or adults at risk
  • donor, beneficiary, staff or volunteer records
  • profiling, scoring or ranking people
  • automated recommendations about people
  • monitoring staff, volunteers, donors or service users
  • large-scale processing
  • new technology in a way people may not expect
  • connected systems such as email, file stores, CRM, HR or case-management tools

If several apply, treat the use as likely to need a DPIA or specialist review.

docs.title: Strong DPIA Triggers
docs.text:
Complete a DPIA before proceeding if the AI use could:

  • affect access to support, grants, employment or services
  • assess risk, vulnerability, eligibility or priority
  • process safeguarding, health, disability or other special category information
  • use personal data in a new or unexpected way
  • connect to a large body of internal files or records
  • create a high risk if the output is wrong, biased or leaked

docs.title: What the DPIA Should Cover
docs.text:
For AI, include:

  • purpose and necessity
  • data categories and sources
  • lawful basis and special-category condition where relevant
  • tool, plan and supplier role
  • retention and deletion
  • training or model-improvement settings
  • connectors and third-party actions
  • international transfers
  • security controls
  • human review
  • bias and accuracy testing
  • incident route
  • review date

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: When do we need to do a DPIA?
  • ICO: Guidance on AI and data protection

docs.title: Before You Paste
docs.text:
Before you paste information into an AI tool, stop and check what the information contains, where it may go, what the tool may do with it, and what could happen if the output is wrong.

This framework does not replace professional advice, a Data Protection Impact Assessment, safeguarding procedures, supplier due diligence or formal organisational approval.

docs.title: The Five-Step Pause
docs.text:
Ask these questions in order:

  1. Is the tool approved for work? If not, stop and use an approved route or ask for approval.
  2. What information are you entering? Check whether it is public, internal, confidential, personal, special category, safeguarding-related or security-sensitive.
  3. Is the information genuinely necessary? Remove, generalise or replace details wherever possible.
  4. What will the AI do? Risk rises when the AI analyses people, recommends decisions, contacts people, searches connected systems or takes actions.
  5. Who checks the result? A named person must be able to understand, challenge and change the output.

A paid personal account is not a work-approved account

Do not assume that paying for an individual AI subscription gives the charity a contract, Data Processing Agreement, admin controls, audit logs, retention controls or suitable data protections.

docs.title: Green: Go Ahead With Normal Checks
docs.text:
Green uses are usually low risk. The information is public, fictional, synthetic or genuinely non-personal. The tool is approved for the task, no significant decision about a person is being made, and a person will review the output.

Examples:

  • brainstorming campaign names
  • rewriting already-public website copy
  • summarising a public report
  • creating a meeting agenda
  • generating fictional examples

Green does not mean the output is automatically accurate.

docs.title: Amber: Pause and Check
docs.text:
Amber uses need more thought before you continue.

Pause when the task involves:

  • personal data
  • internal information
  • unpublished documents
  • donor, staff, volunteer or beneficiary information
  • decisions that could affect people
  • unclear tool settings or account terms
  • connected apps such as email, file storage, CRM, Slack, SharePoint, Google Drive or Notion
  • meeting transcription, donor segmentation, HR drafting or research that may influence policy

Check your charity's policy, speak to the right internal lead, remove unnecessary information, and use an approved route where one exists.

docs.title: Red: Stop
docs.text:
Stop when the information includes:

  • safeguarding case details
  • passwords, API keys or security credentials
  • special category data without an approved arrangement
  • information about children or adults at risk where someone could be identified
  • confidential legal, HR or disciplinary material
  • identifiable beneficiary case notes
  • information for an automated decision that could significantly affect someone
  • autonomous agents that can send messages, publish, delete records, spend money or change systems without approval gates

Use your charity's established safeguarding, data-protection, legal, HR or senior-management process instead.

docs.title: What Meaningful Human Review Means
docs.text:
Human review is not just glancing at the answer.

The reviewer should:

  • understand the task and the risk
  • check facts against reliable sources
  • look for bias, missing context and unsafe wording
  • confirm the output follows charity policy
  • have authority to reject or change the result
  • record the review where the decision is important

docs.title: If You Paste Something by Mistake
docs.text:
If you accidentally enter personal, confidential, safeguarding or security-sensitive information into an AI tool:

  1. Stop using that chat or tool for the task.
  2. Tell your manager or the relevant data-protection, safeguarding or security lead.
  3. Record what was shared, which tool/account was used and when.
  4. Do not try to fix it by pasting more information.
  5. Follow the charity's incident process.

docs.title: Safer Alternatives
docs.text:
When in doubt:

  • use a fictional example
  • remove identifiers
  • summarise the issue at a higher level
  • use public information
  • ask for structure or wording help without sharing the real case
  • use an approved internal tool
  • ask a manager, safeguarding lead or data-protection lead

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Guidance on AI and data protection
  • Charity Commission: Charities and Artificial Intelligence
  • Fundraising Regulator: Guidance for using artificial intelligence in fundraising
  • NCSC: AI and cyber security

docs.title: Everyday Charity Work
docs.text: This section will organise AI guidance around real charity tasks, including communications, fundraising, service delivery, safeguarding, HR, volunteers, finance, governance, research, meetings and digital work.

docs.title: AI in Fundraising
docs.text:
AI can help fundraisers draft, plan, test and summarise. It can also create risks around misleading content, donor privacy, vulnerability, profiling and trust.

The Fundraising Regulator says charitable fundraising institutions remain responsible and accountable for AI use in fundraising, including AI-generated outputs they use.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
Usually suitable with normal review:

  • brainstorming public campaign ideas
  • drafting generic social posts
  • improving the clarity of public copy
  • turning published impact information into a first-draft checklist
  • creating internal planning structures without donor data

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
Pause and check before using AI for:

  • donor segmentation
  • fundraising emails based on supporter data
  • grant applications using internal evidence
  • AI-generated images or stories for public campaigns
  • analysis of survey comments
  • personalised donor communications
  • fundraising content about vulnerable people

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
Do not use a general-purpose or unapproved AI tool for:

  • identifiable donor databases
  • special category data
  • fabricated testimonials or impact evidence
  • synthetic beneficiary stories presented as real
  • decisions about who should receive support, grants or contact without safeguards
  • fundraising approaches that could exploit vulnerability

docs.title: Fundraising AI Checklist
docs.text:
Before using AI:

  • confirm the tool and account are approved
  • assess risk before the use starts
  • check whether donor or supporter data is involved
  • apply data minimisation
  • review for accuracy and fairness
  • check Code of Fundraising Practice expectations
  • decide whether transparency about AI use is needed
  • keep a record of important checks
  • involve trustees for strategic or high-risk use

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Fundraising Regulator: Guidance for using artificial intelligence in fundraising
  • Fundraising Regulator: Data privacy and fundraising
  • Fundraising Regulator: Charitable purposes soft opt-in and fundraising marketing

docs.title: AI Meeting Notes and Transcription
docs.text:
AI meeting notes can save time, but recording and transcription can capture personal data, confidential discussions, special category data and third-party voices.

Do not treat transcription as a default admin feature.

docs.title: Before Recording or Transcribing
docs.text:
Check:

  • the purpose of the recording or transcript
  • who will be recorded
  • whether personal or sensitive topics may arise
  • whether everyone has been given clear notice
  • the lawful and fair basis for processing
  • who can access the transcript and summary
  • how long the recording and transcript will be kept
  • whether the tool/account is approved
  • whether the meeting is too sensitive for AI notes

docs.title: Usually Lower Risk
docs.text:
• public webinar planning
  • routine project meetings with no personal data
  • agenda and action drafting after a human checks the result

docs.title: Higher Risk
docs.text:
• HR meetings
  • complaints
  • safeguarding discussions
  • beneficiary case meetings
  • trustee disputes
  • legal or contract negotiations
  • meetings involving children or vulnerable people

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
• provide advance notice
  • restrict access to recordings and transcripts
  • avoid sensitive meetings unless specifically approved
  • set retention periods
  • check summaries for errors
  • delete recordings when no longer needed
  • record the decision for high-risk meetings

docs.title: Ask for Help When
docs.text:
• participants may feel unable to object
  • special category or safeguarding information may arise
  • the tool stores audio or transcript data outside your control
  • the transcript could affect an employment, safeguarding or service decision

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
Good AI governance should be achievable, proportionate and useful.

Trustees remain responsible for decisions made by their charity. AI can support drafting, research and analysis, but it should not be the sole basis for a material decision.

docs.title: Start With Minimum Controls
docs.text:
Small and medium charities should start with:

  • a named owner for AI use
  • simple staff rules
  • an approved-tools list
  • a basic AI-use register
  • a tool approval process
  • human review expectations
  • an incident route
  • trustee visibility over high-risk use

docs.title: Pages in This Section
docs.text:
• Tool approval
  • Agents and connectors
  • AI incidents

docs.title: Trustee Questions
docs.text:
Trustees can ask:

  • What AI tools are staff already using?
  • Are personal accounts being used for charity work?
  • Which uses involve personal, confidential or safeguarding information?
  • Who approves tools and use cases?
  • What happens if AI output causes harm?
  • How are important decisions checked by humans?

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Charity Commission: Charities and Artificial Intelligence
  • Charity Commission: Safeguarding and protecting people for charities and trustees
  • Fundraising Regulator: Guidance for using artificial intelligence in fundraising

docs.title: Agents and Connectors
docs.text:
Some AI tools do more than answer questions. They can search files, read email, query databases, write to systems, publish content or trigger workflows.

Treat these tools as higher risk than a standalone drafting assistant.

docs.title: Why Risk Increases
docs.text:
Risk increases when AI can:

  • access internal files or email
  • search CRM, HR, finance or case-management systems
  • retrieve confidential information the user did not intend to use
  • follow malicious instructions hidden in documents or webpages
  • send messages, edit records, publish pages or trigger payments
  • expose broadly shared files through normal user permissions

docs.title: Minimum Controls
docs.text:
• start read-only where possible
  • use least privilege
  • restrict connectors to the task
  • audit file and folder permissions before enabling AI search
  • require approval before external actions
  • block access to secrets, credentials and sensitive folders
  • log activity
  • test with failure cases
  • create a kill switch or rollback process
  • review incidents and near misses

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
Do not allow an agent to:

  • send external messages without approval
  • publish content without review
  • delete or change important records without approval
  • access safeguarding, HR or case records without specialist assessment
  • handle money or procurement decisions without controls
  • act on hidden instructions from untrusted content

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• NCSC: Thinking carefully before adopting agentic AI
  • NCSC: AI and cyber security
  • GOV.UK: Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI

docs.title: AI Incidents
docs.text: An AI incident is any AI-related event that may create harm, disclose information, mislead people, affect a decision unfairly or damage trust.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• personal data pasted into an unapproved tool
  • safeguarding details shared with a chatbot
  • confidential files exposed through an AI connector
  • fabricated information published as fact
  • a harmful or biased output used in a decision
  • AI-generated images presented as real evidence
  • an agent sends, deletes or changes something without approval

docs.title: First Steps
docs.text:
1. Stop the use.
  2. Preserve basic facts: tool, account, date, prompt, output and people affected.
  3. Tell the right internal lead.
  4. Do not paste more information into the tool to investigate.
  5. Follow existing data-protection, safeguarding, security or complaints processes.

docs.title: Review Questions
docs.text:
• Was personal or confidential information disclosed?
  • Could a child, beneficiary, staff member, volunteer or donor be harmed?
  • Was a decision affected?
  • Was the output published or shared externally?
  • Can the data be deleted or access restricted?
  • Does the charity need to notify anyone?
  • What control failed?
  • What should change before the tool is used again?

docs.title: Tool Approval
docs.text:
Approve the tool, account plan and use case, not just the brand.

For example, a personal paid AI account is not the same as an organisational workspace with a contract, admin controls, retention settings and audit logs.

docs.title: Minimum Approval Questions
docs.text:
Ask:

  • What problem are we solving?
  • Which tool, plan and account type will be used?
  • What data will be entered or connected?
  • Will the provider use inputs or outputs for model training?
  • What retention and deletion controls exist?
  • Is there a Data Processing Agreement or suitable contract?
  • Where may data be processed or transferred?
  • What admin, SSO, MFA, audit and access controls exist?
  • Can the tool connect to email, files, CRM, HR or case systems?
  • Who owns the use case?
  • Who reviews outputs?
  • What happens if the tool gives harmful or wrong output?
  • When will the approval be reviewed?

docs.title: Minimum AI-Use Register Fields
docs.text: Field Why it matters Use ID Gives the use a stable reference Tool and plan Distinguishes brand from account controls Owner Creates accountability Purpose Reduces function creep People affected Identifies consultation and risk needs Data categories Screens personal and special category data Connectors/actions Shows expanded access Human review Names the reviewer and authority Risk rating Green, Amber or Red DPIA status Shows data-protection governance Approval and review date Supports lifecycle control

docs.title: Approval Outcomes
docs.text:
• Approved: use may proceed within the agreed scope.
  • Approved with limits: use may proceed only with specific controls.
  • Needs specialist review: data protection, safeguarding, legal, HR or procurement review is needed.
  • Not approved: do not use for charity work.

docs.title: Learning Paths
docs.text: This section will curate existing guidance for common roles and starting points, such as new AI users, small charity leaders, trustees, fundraisers, communicators, service-delivery teams and data-protection leads.

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text:
This privacy page is a placeholder for the public site.

The project should minimise personal data collection. Public repositories must never contain safeguarding records, identifiable beneficiary data, employee records, confidential organisational information or raw research responses.

docs.title: Research and Community
docs.text:
This section invites research, feedback and practical collaboration on safer AI use in charities.

AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew James Nel as an open, evidence-led resource for UK charities.

Connect with Matthew on LinkedIn

docs.title: Research Interests
docs.text:
The project welcomes research conversations on:

  • AI policy in charities
  • responsible AI governance for small and medium charities
  • trustee oversight and assurance
  • staff confidence, training and adoption barriers
  • AI use in fundraising, communications, HR, governance and service delivery
  • AI, safeguarding and vulnerable people
  • AI, GDPR and data minimisation in charity workflows
  • approved-tools processes and practical risk assessment

docs.title: Ways to Contribute
docs.text:
You can help by:

  • suggesting sources
  • reviewing draft guidance
  • proposing practical examples
  • sharing anonymised workflow insights
  • testing pages for clarity and accessibility
  • identifying missing topics
  • contributing to research packs

Do not submit personal data, safeguarding case details, confidential charity material or private research responses through public project spaces.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
AI can help charities draft, summarise, plan and learn. It can also create privacy, safeguarding, accuracy and accountability risks when used without clear checks.

Start with three rules:

  1. Do not paste personal, confidential or safeguarding information into an unapproved AI tool.
  2. Use the least information needed for the task.
  3. Keep a human responsible for the output and the final decision.

Recommended first pages:

  • Before You Paste
  • AI and Data Protection
  • Everyday Charity Work
  • Governance

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
This section covers tool evaluation and guidance for common AI products.

Tool guidance must be checked against current provider documentation before procurement or rollout. Product terms, retention settings, training settings and admin controls change frequently.

docs.title: Core Rule
docs.text:
Do not approve a brand name alone. Approve the tool, account plan, settings and use case.

For example, a personal paid account may not provide the same protections as a business or enterprise workspace.

docs.title: Tool Pages
docs.text:
• Account plans and data controls
  • ChatGPT
  • Claude
  • Google Gemini
  • Microsoft Copilot
  • Notion AI
  • Perplexity
  • Canva AI
  • Adobe Firefly

docs.title: Every Tool Review Checks
docs.text:
• what the tool does
  • plan and account differences
  • whether inputs may be used for model training
  • retention and deletion controls
  • admin controls
  • connectors and actions
  • suitable charity uses
  • Green, Amber and Red use cases
  • human review requirements
  • known limitations

docs.title: Account Plans and Data Controls
docs.text:
The tool brand alone does not tell you whether a charity use is appropriate.

Different account types can have different:

  • contracts
  • Data Processing Agreements
  • model-training settings
  • retention settings
  • admin controls
  • audit logs
  • connectors
  • deletion processes
  • support arrangements

docs.title: Personal Accounts
docs.text: Use personal accounts only for public, non-confidential Green tasks unless the charity has explicitly assessed and approved the use.

docs.title: Organisational Accounts
docs.text:
Business, team, education, charity or enterprise plans may provide stronger controls, but they still need a use-case assessment.

Check:

  • current official documentation
  • contract and DPA
  • admin settings
  • training and feedback settings
  • retention and deletion
  • connectors and third-party apps
  • audit access
  • who owns the workspace

docs.title: Do Not Assume
docs.text:
• paid means approved
  • no training means no retention
  • enterprise means every use is safe
  • user deletion means immediate back-end deletion
  • citations mean a source has been checked
  • connectors are safe because the user already had access

docs.title: Practical Approval Rule
docs.text:
Write approvals like this:

Tool: ChatGPT Business. Use case: drafting public campaign copy from public information. Data: no personal or confidential data. Reviewer: Communications Manager. Review date: October 2026.

Avoid approvals like this:

ChatGPT is approved.

docs.title: Adobe Firefly
docs.text: Adobe Firefly can generate and edit images, vectors, video, audio and design assets.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• generic illustrations and textures
  • background extension or removal on assets the charity owns
  • concept development
  • non-personal campaign assets reviewed by a designer

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• synthetic people or emotionally sensitive scenes
  • editing images of real beneficiaries
  • third-party or partner models
  • campaign assets where rights, publicity or trademark issues matter

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• false documentary imagery presented as a real event
  • changing safeguarding or incident evidence
  • uploading material without rights
  • deepfake audio or video of a real person

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Adobe Firefly FAQ
  • Adobe: Partner models in Firefly Enterprise
  • Adobe for Nonprofits

docs.title: Canva AI
docs.text: Canva AI and Magic features can support design, social content, presentations, images and video.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• public campaign concepting
  • generic backgrounds and illustrative assets
  • accessible social templates with human editing
  • resizing and adapting approved public content

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• synthetic people in fundraising or service communications
  • uploading brand, partner or licensed assets
  • depictions of sensitive experiences
  • AI voice, video or translation intended for public use

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• fictional beneficiary stories presented as real
  • identifiable case images without rights and approval
  • impersonating a real person
  • medical, crisis or safeguarding messaging without specialist review

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Canva AI Product Terms
  • Canva: Manage privacy settings
  • Canva for Nonprofits

docs.title: ChatGPT
docs.text: ChatGPT can help with writing, editing, summarising, planning, research support, coding and template drafting.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• public-copy drafting
  • summarising public reports with source checks
  • code or spreadsheet help using dummy data
  • generic templates and checklists

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• internal documents in an approved Business or Enterprise workspace
  • connected apps after permission review
  • minimised or pseudonymised data with documented approval
  • custom GPTs or actions with controlled knowledge and permissions

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• safeguarding or case records in personal accounts
  • secrets, passwords or API keys
  • autonomous beneficiary advice
  • significant decisions without safeguards
  • assuming Temporary Chat makes an otherwise unsafe disclosure acceptable

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• OpenAI: Enterprise privacy
  • OpenAI: Data controls FAQ
  • OpenAI: Temporary Chat FAQ
  • OpenAI for Nonprofits

docs.title: Claude
docs.text: Claude can support writing, document analysis, research, coding and sustained project work.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• public writing and editing
  • analysing public reports
  • coding against non-sensitive repositories
  • policy outlines without confidential inputs

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• internal documents in Claude for Work under an assessed contract
  • projects containing internal knowledge
  • connectors after repository and permission review
  • enterprise use where retention is configured

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• consumer accounts for beneficiary or HR personal data
  • feedback submission on sensitive conversations
  • broad agent permissions to local files or systems
  • assuming deletion is immediate in every back-end system

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Anthropic: Is my data used for model training?
  • Anthropic: How long do you store my organisation's data?
  • Claude Team and Enterprise plans
  • Claude for Nonprofits

docs.title: Google Gemini
docs.text: Google Gemini may be used as a personal app or through Google Workspace. These are not the same governance situation.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• drafting from public information
  • summarising non-sensitive Workspace material in an approved tenant
  • producing internal templates with no personal data

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• Gmail, Drive or Meet integration
  • meeting transcription and summaries
  • confidential internal documents
  • Workspace use where admin controls and retention need review

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• work data through a personal Gemini account
  • broad Drive access without a permission audit
  • beneficiary decisions based on Gemini output
  • case or safeguarding records in an unapproved account

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Google: Gemini Apps Privacy Hub
  • Google: Use Gemini Apps with a work or school account
  • Google Workspace for Nonprofits

docs.title: Microsoft Copilot
docs.text: Microsoft Copilot can work across Microsoft 365, including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, Teams, SharePoint and agents.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• drafting ordinary documents
  • summarising approved internal documents the user needs for their role
  • meeting/action summaries in an approved tenant and process

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• Teams meeting summaries
  • agents grounded in SharePoint or connected data
  • analysis of HR, donor or beneficiary information
  • web grounding for confidential topics

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• using Copilot instead of fixing overshared SharePoint or OneDrive permissions
  • autonomous sending, deletion or external publication without approval
  • significant employment or service decisions without safeguards

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Microsoft: Data, privacy and security for Microsoft 365 Copilot
  • Microsoft: Web search in Microsoft 365 Copilot
  • Microsoft AI for nonprofits

docs.title: Notion AI
docs.text: Notion AI can draft, search a workspace, summarise material, support meeting notes and work with connected apps.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• drafting from public or non-sensitive workspace pages
  • project summaries without personal information
  • knowledge-base search where permissions are already sound

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• meeting transcription
  • connected Google Drive or Slack search
  • agents that edit pages or databases
  • confidential strategy or governance material

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• using AI to expose pages that were accidentally over-shared
  • safeguarding or case information without specific approval
  • silently recording participants
  • autonomous edits to critical registers without review

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Notion: AI security and privacy practices
  • Notion: AI Meeting Notes
  • Notion for Nonprofits

docs.title: Perplexity
docs.text: Perplexity can help discover sources, compare public guidance and produce cited research drafts.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• finding current public sources
  • creating search maps and reading lists
  • comparing public guidance before opening and checking each source

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• uploading internal documents in approved Enterprise use
  • policy research where citations influence public guidance
  • enterprise knowledge connectors
  • research on sensitive organisational topics

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• treating generated citations as verified evidence
  • uploading personal data to consumer plans
  • copying long copyrighted passages
  • publishing unverified legal or safeguarding conclusions

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Perplexity: Data collection
  • Perplexity: Audit logs
  • Perplexity: Enterprise onboarding guide

docs.title: Updates
docs.text:
This section records what changed, what is planned and what still needs review.

  • Changelog
  • Roadmap

docs.title: July 2026 Research Update
docs.text:
This update converts the first research batch into draft public guidance and navigation.

The research pack reviewed UK charity AI safety, data protection, safeguarding, governance, fundraising and common AI tools.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• DPIA triage for AI
  • Interim automated-decision and profiling guidance
  • AI fundraising guidance
  • Meeting notes and transcription guidance
  • Tool approval guidance
  • Agents and connectors guidance
  • AI incident guidance
  • Account-plan and data-control guidance
  • Draft tool guides for ChatGPT, Claude, Google Gemini, Microsoft Copilot, Notion AI, Perplexity, Canva AI and Adobe Firefly

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• Before You Paste now uses a five-step pause: tool approval, data category, necessity, AI action and human review.
  • AI and Data Protection now reflects the July 2026 DUAA position at a high level.
  • Governance now highlights trustee accountability and minimum controls.
  • FAQ and Glossary now include core AI governance terms and practical questions.

docs.title: Human Review Required
docs.text:
The following remain draft or interim pending named review:

  • automated-decision legal wording
  • special category and criminal-offence examples
  • meeting transcription lawful-basis examples
  • safeguarding and beneficiary-facing AI use
  • recruitment and HR use cases
  • donor profiling and soft opt-in scenarios
  • provider suitability for personal-data processing

docs.title: Research Inputs
docs.text:
• monthly-update-packs/2026-07/AI_SAFE_CHARITY_INDEX_COMPLETE_RESEARCH_PACK_2026-07.md
  • monthly-update-packs/2026-07/UK-Charity-AI-Safety-Landscape-and-Tool-Implementation-Report-2026-07.md

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: The Data Use and Access Act 2025: what does it mean for organisations?
  • ICO: Technology guidance plan
  • Fundraising Regulator: Guidance for using artificial intelligence in fundraising
  • NCSC: Thinking carefully before adopting agentic AI

Foundation build:

  • MkDocs Material site foundation
  • task-based navigation skeleton
  • Before You Paste starter guidance
  • repository governance files
  • GitHub Pages deployment workflow

Known limitations:

  • most guidance pages are section stubs
  • source and claim registers are not yet published
  • frontmatter validation is not yet automated

docs.title: July 2026 Research Update - 2026-07-12
docs.text:
Added draft pages for DPIA triage, automated decisions, AI fundraising, meeting transcription, tool approval, agents/connectors, AI incidents and common AI tool reviews.

Updated Before You Paste, AI and Data Protection, Governance, Tools, FAQ and Glossary using the July 2026 research batch.

Known limitations:

  • automated-decision guidance remains interim pending final ICO guidance
  • high-risk GDPR, safeguarding, HR and fundraising scenarios require human or specialist review
  • provider claims must be rechecked against current official documentation before procurement

docs.title: Phase 1: Foundation
docs.text:
• Create the MkDocs site foundation.
  • Publish the task-based navigation skeleton.
  • Add repository governance files.
  • Publish the first Before You Paste guidance.

docs.title: Phase 2: Core Guidance
docs.text:
• Expand Before You Paste.
  • Add AI and data-protection guidance.
  • Add safeguarding and trustee guidance.
  • Add charity workflow pages.

docs.title: Phase 3: Tools and Templates
docs.text:
• Publish the tool evaluation framework.
  • Add current tool guides.
  • Add policy, register and review templates.

docs.title: Phase 4: Quality Gates
docs.text:
• Add frontmatter validation.
  • Add link checking.
  • Add review-date reporting.
  • Publish evidence and claim registers.
